title: Caffeine Dosage Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Optimal Caffeine Dosage for Athletic Performance
description: Calculate your ideal caffeine dosage for better athletic performance by body weight. Based on ISSN & EFSA guidelines. Free, instant results.
seoKeywords: caffeine mg per kg body weight
intro: Reference values in sports nutrition come from population-based studies (ISSN, EFSA). They provide a framework to interpret your results, but your personal context matters most. Calculate your ideal caffeine dosage to enhance athletic performance based on your body weight. Based on ISSN and EFSA guidelines. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you informed.
keyTakeaway: **Optimal dose**: 3–6 mg/kg, 30–60 min before workout. For 75 kg = **225–450 mg** (~2–4 cups). Don't exceed 400 mg/day (EFSA). Tolerance develops in 1–2 weeks of daily use.
useCases: You want to know how many mg of caffeine to take before training.
You need to calculate if your pre-workout supplement has the right dosage.
You want to know how many cups of coffee equal an athletic dose.
You're sensitive to caffeine and looking for the minimum effective dose.
You're planning caffeine cycling for competition.

faq.q: How much caffeine is in a cup of coffee?
faq.a: An **espresso**: ~63 mg. A **cup of filtered coffee** (8 oz): ~95 mg. A **mate** (full thermos): ~200–300 mg total. A **pre-workout supplement**: 150–400 mg.

faq.q: When should I take caffeine before my workout?
faq.a: **30–60 minutes before** is optimal. Peak caffeine levels occur 45–60 minutes after intake. For capsules, aim for 60 min; for coffee, 30–45 min.
**Minimally.** Caffeine's diuretic effect is mild and easily offset by drinking water. You don't need to worry about dehydration from pre-workout caffeine.
If you're using it as an ergogenic aid, yes. **Take 2 weeks without caffeine** before competition to resensitize your receptors and maximize the effect on race day.

faq.q: Is more caffeine always better for performance?
faq.a: **No.** Above 6 mg/kg, side effects (anxiety, heart palpitations, tremors) outweigh benefits. The dose-response curve has a ceiling.
Yes. Caffeine has a **half-life of 5–6 hours**. If you train at 6 PM and take caffeine, it can disrupt your sleep. Avoid caffeine **within 8 hours** of bedtime.

faq.q: Can caffeine help me burn fat?
faq.a: It slightly increases energy expenditure (**3–11%**) and fat oxidation. But the effect is modest—it doesn't replace a calorie deficit for weight loss.

faq.q: When should I talk to a doctor about caffeine?
faq.a: If you have values outside normal ranges, experience symptoms, or have a family history of cardiovascular or metabolic disease.

explanation:
**Reference values in health** come from large population-based studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. They're guidelines; your personal medical history always takes precedence.

## How it's calculated

Calculate your ideal caffeine dosage to enhance athletic performance based on your body weight. Based on ISSN and EFSA guidelines.

This calculator applies the formula with the values you enter, checking typical ranges and alerting you to any unusual values.

## Normal reference ranges

| Parameter | Normal adult range |
|---|---|
| Blood pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting heart rate | 60–100 bpm |
| Temperature | 36.1–37.2°C |
| SpO2 | 95–100% |
| Fasting glucose | 70–100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL | >40 (M) / >50 (W) |

## Final notes

This calculation is a reference guide. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a specialist. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.
